title: Fanconi anemia: matched unrelated donor BMT
vignette: A 7-year-old boy is admitted to the hospital after his adoptive parents brought him to the emergency department due to fatigue and pallor. Laboratory evaluation reveals pancytopenia with an absolute neutrophil count consistent with severe neutropenia. Bone marrow examination reveals signs of aplastic anemia, and subsequent genetic testing reveals a congenital genomic defect in DNA repair leading to chromosomal instability. A bone marrow transplant is required for treatment. The patient's only living relatives are an identical twin brother and older sister who were both adopted by the same parents. He also has stored umbilical stem cells that were harvested after birth. Which of the following is the best bone marrow transplantation method for this patient?

clues.text: congenital genomic defect in DNA repair leading to chromosomal instability
clues.note: A congenital DNA-repair defect with chromosomal instability and aplastic anemia in a child describes Fanconi anemia. Critically, this germline defect is present in EVERY cell of the patient — and in an identical twin.
An identical (monozygotic) twin would be a perfect HLA match (syngeneic), but he carries the SAME inherited DNA-repair defect — so his marrow would reintroduce the disease. This is the key trap.

clues.text: older sister who were both adopted by the same parents
clues.note: 'Adopted by the same parents' means the sister is NOT genetically related, so she is unlikely to be HLA-matched (and not a reliable sibling donor). It removes the usual matched-sibling option.

clues.text: stored umbilical stem cells that were harvested after birth
clues.note: The patient's own banked cord blood is autologous — but it carries the same congenital Fanconi defect, so it can't cure the disease. Another decoy.

whatMatters: This is Fanconi anemia (congenital DNA-repair defect). Any source carrying the patient's genome — his identical twin (syngeneic), his own stored cord blood (autologous) — also carries the disease and can't cure it. The adopted sister isn't genetically related (no reliable HLA match). So the best option is a MATCHED UNRELATED DONOR (healthy allogeneic graft).
strategy: For transplanting an inherited (germline) bone marrow disease, exclude any donor source that shares the patient's genome: autologous and identical-twin (syngeneic) grafts carry the same defect. You need a healthy ALLOGENEIC donor — a matched related donor if available, otherwise a matched unrelated donor.

options.text: Stored umbilical cord stem cells from the patient

eliminations.label: autologous (fresh) — carries the same defect
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an autologous transplant uses the patient's OWN marrow/stem cells. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the patient's cells harbor the congenital DNA-repair defect, so reinfusing them reintroduces Fanconi anemia — no cure. ✅ Correct if: the disease were acquired (e.g., a chemo-sensitive lymphoma) where the patient's own stem cells are healthy.

eliminations.label: stored cord blood (patient's own) — autologous, same defect
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the patient's banked umbilical cord stem cells are also autologous. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: like the fresh autologous graft, these cells carry the inherited defect and won't cure the disease. ✅ Correct if: the condition were an acquired marrow failure/malignancy with genetically normal banked cells.

eliminations.label: identical twin (syngeneic) — perfect match but same disease (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a syngeneic transplant from a monozygotic twin is a perfect HLA match with no graft-versus-host disease. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: an identical twin shares the SAME germline DNA-repair defect, so his marrow would also be Fanconi-affected — curing nothing. This is the central trap. ✅ Correct if: the patient's disease were ACQUIRED (not inherited), where an identical-twin graft would be ideal.

eliminations.label: unmatched sibling allogeneic — poor HLA match
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an allogeneic graft from a sibling who is NOT HLA-matched. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the 'sister' is adopted (not genetically related) and unmatched, so the graft risks rejection and severe graft-versus-host disease — worse than a matched unrelated donor. ✅ Correct if: a genetically related sibling were confirmed HLA-matched (matched related donor, the usual first choice).

reasoning: Congenital DNA-repair defect + aplastic anemia in a child = Fanconi anemia (germline disease)
Autologous and identical-twin (syngeneic) sources carry the same defect → can't cure
The adopted sister isn't genetically related (unmatched/unsuitable)
Best = healthy allogeneic graft from a matched unrelated donor → answer B
mechanism: Fanconi anemia is an autosomal recessive DNA-repair (interstrand cross-link) disorder causing chromosomal instability, progressive bone marrow failure (aplastic anemia/pancytopenia), congenital anomalies, and cancer risk. Because the defect is in the germline, it is present in all of the patient's cells AND in a monozygotic twin, so autologous or syngeneic transplants reintroduce diseased stem cells. Cure requires replacing the marrow with genetically NORMAL allogeneic stem cells — ideally an HLA-matched related donor, or, when none exists, a matched unrelated donor.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Heme/Immuno > hematopoietic stem cell transplant types (autologous, syngeneic, allogeneic; matched related vs unrelated) and Fanconi anemia (DNA-repair defect, aplastic anemia, chromosomal instability).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Recognize the disease is GERMLINE: exclude any graft that shares the patient's genome (autologous, identical twin). Then choose the best HEALTHY allogeneic source available (matched unrelated donor here).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on autologous vs syngeneic vs allogeneic, HLA matching, Fanconi anemia, chromosomal instability, graft-versus-host disease? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The identical-twin (syngeneic) option is the dominant trap — a perfect MATCH but the twin shares the SAME inherited defect. For inherited marrow disease, perfect-match-but-same-genome sources can't cure.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The reference is the HLA-inheritance diagram (parental haplotypes; sibling match probabilities). Use it to see why a genetically unrelated 'sister' won't match and why a matched unrelated donor is needed.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Only 33% nationally — the lesson is: inherited (germline) disease → never autologous/identical-twin; you need a healthy allogeneic donor.

keywords.front: Best BMT for an inherited (germline) marrow disease
keywords.back: Healthy allogeneic donor (matched related, else matched unrelated)

keywords.front: Why identical-twin (syngeneic) graft fails in Fanconi anemia
keywords.back: Twin carries the same germline DNA-repair defect
Patient's own cells carry the inherited defect
AR DNA-repair (cross-link) defect; aplastic anemia, chromosomal instability, cancer risk

firstAid.detail: Heme/Immuno > transplant types: autologous vs syngeneic vs allogeneic (matched related/unrelated), HLA matching, GVHD — choosing donors for inherited vs acquired disease.
firstAid.location: Heme/Immuno > transplant types: autologous vs syngeneic vs allogeneic (matched related/unrelated), HLA matching, GVHD — choosing donors for inherited vs acquired disease.

firstAid.detail: Heme > inherited bone marrow failure: Fanconi anemia (DNA cross-link repair defect, aplastic anemia, chromosomal instability, congenital anomalies, malignancy risk).
firstAid.location: Heme > inherited bone marrow failure: Fanconi anemia (DNA cross-link repair defect, aplastic anemia, chromosomal instability, congenital anomalies, malignancy risk).

firstAid.detail: Immunology > HLA inheritance and matching; graft-versus-host disease and rejection.
firstAid.location: Immunology > HLA inheritance and matching; graft-versus-host disease and rejection.

memoryHook: Germline disease = even the identical twin and your own cord blood are 'infected' by the gene → need a HEALTHY outsider: matched unrelated donor.
